evidence: | Day 110: Angel Tie – Aka Tenshi shibari (angel tie). Pull the two elbows in the back to create wings. Note that the tension on the arm should be very loose in order to make this tie safe. The pattern showed is based on a design by Tifereth. Inspirations and Resources |
expected_answer: Keep the tension on the arms very loose; Rope365 says this is necessary to make the tie safer.
expected_question: How should arm tension be set in Rope365's Angel Tie, and why?
reason: A one-line pattern caption cannot establish that very loose arm tension makes this demanding elbow position safe; stronger dataset rows teach range-of-motion assessment, pain boundaries, monitoring, and rapid exit.

answer: Avoid high compression at the upper back of the calf and keep monitoring the tied person's foot movement.
evidence: Day 306: Behind the Knee – Create a trapeze by trapping the pole behind the knees and play with moving the pole around to move the body with it. Note that the some nerves are vulnerable at the top of the back of the calf, avoid high compression in this location and monitor foot movement.
expected_answer: Avoid high compression at the top of the back of the calf and monitor foot movement because nerves there are vulnerable.
expected_question: What lower-leg safety guidance does Rope365 give for a pole trapped behind the knees?
paraphrase_rationale: The replacement preserves the evidence's directly actionable pressure and movement checks without turning its anatomy assertion into the answer.
question: What observable precautions does Rope365 give when a pole is trapped behind the knees?

answer: Use in-person learning for tactile subtleties when available, use books and videos to explore and get started, and evaluate every resource critically because rope knowledge continues to evolve.
evidence: Rope knowledge is infinite and there are many sources of information in various formats. In this section, you will find a variety of resources where you can learn more about rope bondage, shibari, kinbaku. Learning in person is the best way to discover rope as certain concepts requires to feel and touch the rope to understand the subtleties. That said, books, videos and other online content are great to explore the diversity of the rope world and can help get you started if you don’t have the privilege of a local community. Always look at the different resources with critical thinking. Rope is a rather recent practice in history and we are constantly learning to make it safer.
expected_answer: Rope is a rather recent practice in history and we are constantly learning to make it safer.
expected_question: Why does Rope365 recommend critical thinking when using rope resources?
paraphrase_rationale: The replacement captures the evidence's practical division of labor among in-person, book, and video learning plus its critical-thinking instruction.
question: How does Rope365 recommend combining in-person and online rope learning?
reason: The replacement captures the evidence's practical division of labor among in-person, book, and video learning plus its critical-thinking instruction.

evidence: Many different materials can be used to make rope. Plants like cotton, jute or hemp are quite common rope material, and so are synthetic fibers like nylon, polyester, or MFP. There are also other organic materials like silk or bamboo that are less common but have very interesting properties. Some materials will handle tying knots better than others, some can be unsafe to use for bondage as they contain chemicals that are bad for the skin, others will provide unique sensations. Expanding our understanding of the diversity of rope materials and what makes each of them special will guide us in finding the perfect rope for the occasion.
expected_answer: some can be unsafe to use for bondage as they contain chemicals that are bad for the skin
expected_question: Why does Rope365 say rope material choice can affect bondage safety?
reason: The source names neither a material nor a chemical and provides no way to evaluate the claim; retained material rows give specific construction, handling, and care tradeoffs.
